data.title: TNYA Pulls Back After Hours Following Significant $1.13B Deal-Spurred Rally
data.description:
Key points:
  • Under the deal, Tenaya Therapeutics will validate up to 15 gene targets and receive as much as $1.13 billion in development and commercial milestone payments from Alnylam.
  • Alnylam will be responsible for all development and commercialization activities of therapeutics associated with the identified gene targets, the companies said.

Shares of Tenaya Therapeutics, Inc. (TNYA) pulled back after hours after a significant rally during the regular session on Thursday after the company said that it has entered into a research collaboration deal with Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) to potentially develop drugs for cardiovascular disease.

TNYA shares pulled back 5% in extended hours of trading after closing up 41% in the regular session.

Deal For Heart Drugs

Under the deal, Tenaya Therapeutics will validate up to 15 gene targets and receive as much as $1.13 billion in development and commercial milestone payments from Alnylam if all the targets lead to approved therapies for cardiovascular disease. Tenaya will receive up to $10 million in the form of an upfront payment, the company said.

“By combining our modality agnostic target identification and validation capabilities with Alnylam’s leadership in RNA interference therapeutics, we have an opportunity to advance candidates for novel genetic targets with the potential to create transformational medicines for patients with heart disease,” Tenaya CEO Faraz Ali said.

Alnylam will be responsible for all development and commercialization activities of therapeutics associated with the identified gene targets, the companies said.

How Did Stocktwits Users React?

On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around TNYA stock rose from ‘bearish’ to ‘bullish’ over the past 24 hours, while message volume increased from ‘normal’ to ‘high’ levels.

A Stocktwits user said that they are awaiting positive data for Tenaya’s TN401 this year with “no curative competition.” TN-401 is a gene therapy for a heart condition called PKP2-associated arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC).

https://www.stocktwits.com/Raumerica/message/646689743

Another said that the best part about the deal is that Tenaya will just be researching fifteen additional targets for future commercialisation while still owning all of its own drugs.

https://www.stocktwits.com/Raumerica/message/646703938

TNYA stock has gained 78% over the past 12 months.

data.title: South Korea Consumer Prices Rose 2.0% in February — Update
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

South Korean headline inflation matched the central bank's annual target for a second straight month in February, supporting the Bank of Korea's extended pause in rate cuts.

The benchmark consumer-price index rose 2.0% from a year earlier, the same pace as in January, the Ministry of Data and Statistics said Friday.

The latest reading was just below the median forecast of 13 economists in a Wall Street Journal poll, which had penciled in a 2.1% increase.

On a month-to-month basis, consumer prices rose 0.3% in February, slightly below the poll's forecast for a 0.4% increase. In January, prices rose 0.4% on month.

Core CPI, which strips out volatile food and energy prices, rose 2.3% from a year earlier in February. It gained 0.4% from the previous month.

Ministry data showed that prices of energy and industrial goods largely declined in February, offsetting inflationary pressure from higher agricultural and livestock prices.

The Bank of Korea in February held its policy rate steady for a sixth straight meeting and signaled no change during the next six months after slightly raising its 2026 inflation forecast.

The central bank now expects inflation to average 2.2% this year, up from its previous projection of 2.1%, before easing to 2.0% next year.

With inflation largely subdued and exports resilient amid strong chip demand, policymakers are widely expected to refrain from further rate cuts.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: ONEnergy Inc. Reports 2025 Annual Results And Provides Corporate Update
data.description:
(TheNewswire)

    TORONTO, CANADA (March 5,2026) - TheNewswire– ONEnergy Inc.
    (“ONEnergy” or the "Company”) (NEX: OEG.H), today announced
    that it has filed its financial results as at and for the year ended
    December 31, 2025 and provides a corporate update.


    Financial results


    For the three-month period ended December 31, 2025, net
    loss and comprehensive loss was ($193,000) compared to net loss and
    comprehensive loss of ($138,000), during the same period in
    2024.


    For the year ended December 31, 2025, net loss and
    comprehensive loss was ($628,000) compared to net income and
    comprehensive income of $9,867,000, during the year ended December 31,
    2024.


    Net income and comprehensive income for the year ended
    December 31, 2024 included a gain on settlement of unsecured
    liabilities of $10,480,000 related to a Division I proposal
    implemented pursuant to the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act (Canada) on June
    26, 2024.


    For further information on the financial results of the
    Company, please review the Company’s audited consolidated financial
    statements and management’s discussion and analysis of financial
    condition and results of operations for the years ended December 31,
    2025 and 2024, available under the Company’s issuer profile
    on SEDAR+ at www.sedarplus.ca.

    Secured grid promissory note debtfinancing

    Between November 26, 2025 and March 5, 2026, StephenJ.J. Letwin, a director and Chairman of the Board of Directors,provided advances under a secured grid promissory note (the“Secured Note”) to the Company totaling $38,890.  The Secured Note is not convertibleinto securities of the Company, is secured by a first-ranking securityover the Company’s assets, permits repayments and additionaldrawdowns and bears an annual interest rate at 10%.

    About ONEnergy Inc.

    ONEnergy common shares are listed on the NEX board ofthe TSX Venture Exchange under the symbol “OEG.H”.  Materialinformation about ONEnergy can be found on SEDAR+ under theCompany’s issuer profile at www.sedarplus.ca. ONEnergy's corporate website may be found at www.onenergyinc.com.

data.title: How a Father-Son Advisor Team Decided to Sell Their Practice to Carson Wealth — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Weld Royal

For Nate Fisher and his father, the decision to sell their New England wealth management practice followed years of succession planning and careful evaluation — not a quick reaction to industry headlines about the high prices sellers could fetch. The pair began working together in 2015, created their own firm in 2017, and expanded the business so much that they faced a strategic question: whether to try to hire other talent to meet the needs of their growing client base or join forces with another firm. On our latest The Way Forward: Next Generation podcast , Fisher, who is now a managing director at Carson Wealth, talks about the difficulties of finding talent and specialized expertise in small town America, why that motivated him and his father to explore a sale of their firm, and how they went about choosing Carson Wealth.

Small firm succession challenges. "Being in a rural town has strengths and weaknesses," Fisher says. "And one of those was actually finding talent." In addition, Fisher knew he would eventually take over the business, and succession planning introduced financial considerations. As his father approached retirement, the firm had to confront how a buyout would be structured and funded. Fisher recalls thinking about "what sort of debt is this company going to take on essentially to have my dad retire?"

Rather than make a quick decision, the two men hired a consultant to explore options such as strategic partnerships and outright acquisition. They learned about local and national firms interested in a tie-up and began interviewing them. "It took us quite a long time, and I think really years," Fisher says.

Culture and client fit. Cultural alignment and client continuity rose to the top of their criteria. The Fishers had built their business around longstanding relationships, and they wanted to protect those connections and the firm's local identity. Fisher says they worried about clients feeling that the firm's affiliation with a larger organization meant losing access, personalization, or trust. "We did not want our clients to all of sudden feel like we changed," he explains.

Fisher recalls being reassured by Carson Wealth that the firm's approach to client service would remain intact. He also liked the resources and additional offerings clients would be able to access through the partnership. That combination pushed the Fishers to strike a deal with Carson Wealth. "We have more to offer the clients now. There's more services and those keep expanding, which is wonderful. But the look and feel and the relationship we have with our clients has not changed," he says.

The heavy lift of implementation. The hard work of implementation followed the partnership decision. The transition to Carson Wealth involved compliance reviews, due diligence, privacy policies, and extensive paperwork. Technology shifts created additional strain. "If you're changing all of your software in your company, there's a learning curve there, too," says Fisher. He warns that preparation is critical. "You're going to want to make sure that your team that you have in place now is ready for that lift."

Fisher says team members needed to understand that workloads would spike and processes would change. "You want to make sure that everybody's excited and prepared and that you're all pulling the rope in the same direction," he says.

Fisher recalls client reaction to the Carson Wealth partnership news being a top worry and focused communication around reassurance, rather than new services. "You want to talk about how your relationship is not going to change." For advisors contemplating a sale or partnership, Fisher recommends taking the time to find the right fit — and being ready for the work that follows.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Canada's Canfor reports Q4 operating loss due to lumber tariffs
data.description:
Overview

  • Canada's forest products firm reported Q4 operating loss of C$415.9 mln due to market pressures

  • Adjusted operating loss for Q4 widened to C$145 mln from Q3

  • Lumber production rose 2% from Q3, aided by Hedin sawmills acquisition

Outlook

  • Canfor anticipates continued uncertainty in North American lumber markets through 2026

  • Offshore lumber market conditions in Asia forecast to remain subdued in Q1 2026

  • CPPI may experience continued declines in financial performance in Q1 2026

Result Drivers

  • LUMBER MARKET PRESSURE - Elevated US softwood lumber duties and tariffs contributed to weak demand and increased operating losses, according to CEO Susan Yurkovich

  • EUROPEAN OPERATIONS - Lumber production increased 2% from Q3, driven by full quarter contribution from Hedin sawmills in Europe

  • PULP MARKET CONDITIONS - Global softwood pulp markets remained flat due to high inventory levels, impacting pulp segment performance

Company press release:

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 3 "strong buy" or "buy", 3 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the forest & wood products peer group is "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Canfor Corp is C$15.50, about 14.1% above its March 5 closing price of C$13.58
